summary: Gruber corrects his earlier criticism of Safari's scriptability, demonstrating that Apple's new browser is actually fairly scriptable through its Safari suite, though it lacks standard web browser Apple event commands like OpenURL. He walks through the differences between scripting Safari and Internet Explorer via AppleScript, noting Cocoa's unintuitive syntax requirements. The post culminates in a practical AppleScript that lists all open Safari URLs in a dialog and inserts the selected one into BBEdit, streamlining his link-insertion workflow.
key_points: Safari lacks standard web browser Apple event commands (OpenURL/GetURL) but is scriptable through its own Safari suite with document objects
Cocoa apps require unintuitive 'at end of documents' syntax for creating new documents via AppleScript, contributing to AppleScript's reputation as difficult
Gruber shares a practical script to pull URLs from open Safari windows into BBEdit for faster hyperlink insertion
The Safari scripting approach using document properties differs significantly from IE's more straightforward OpenURL command
money_quotes: Ends up that criticism was somewhat misguided — Safari actually is fairly scriptable.
Unintuitive superfluous syntax like this, combined with unhelpful error messages, is what gives AppleScript a reputation for being hard to write.
And a rather unhelpful error, at that: "An error of type 6 has occurred."
In what should come as a surprise to no one, I do all of my Fireball writing in BBEdit.
I'm not sure why the "at end of documents" part is necessary, but scripts won't work without it.
key_insight: Safari's scriptability is real but hampered by Cocoa's unintuitive AppleScript conventions and unhelpful error messages, making it functional yet frustrating compared to IE's more straightforward scripting dictionary.
